# Pr 176421 Coalescing Invoker Grace Period And Structured Output

Source type: github
Document ID: dsid_c7860fdbc17a48b0b6c5f9a0c5e3d054
Release: v1.0.0
Benchmark: EnterpriseRAG-Bench synthetic upstream data
Coalescing Invoker: grace-period batching, deterministic injection, and structured-output streaming compatibility

Context: Multi-tool call patterns at scale expose a tradeoff between per-request latency and downstream execution overhead when we naively fan out tool invocations. Customers reported jitter in streaming handshakes when several tool results were injected concurrently (see ENG-8921). Motivation: Smooth out spikes from parallel tool warmups and avoid duplicate retries while preserving deterministic injection order (for structured output consumers and OpenAI tool-calling compatibility). This PR implements a lightweight coalescing invoker that introduces a configurable grace period (default 10ms) to allow closely timed tool calls from the same top-level request to batch their execution, plus deterministic merge points for tool-result injection and a coordinated retry protocol that reduces duplicate tool executions. Key changes and design: 1) Grace-window coalescer (api/invoker/coalescer.py): collects tool call intents for a single request context for up to grace_period_ms before dispatching a prioritized execution set. Grace window is short (default 10ms) to keep tail latency bounded; tunable via request-level config. 2) Deterministic mergepoints (runtime/invoker/mergepoint.go): tool results are assigned stable sequence slots at dispatch time so downstream structured output assembly is order-deterministic even if tools finish out-of-order. This preserves existing structured-output expectations and OpenAI function-call ordering semantics. 3) Coordinated retry token (protocol/invoker_retry.proto + runtime): introduces a small idempotency token coupled with a coordinator lease to avoid concurrent retries launching duplicate side effects when multiple workers observe timeouts. Token acquisition is optimistic; fallback retry uses backoff and escalates to a single-quorum retry owner. 4) Streaming handshake stabilizations (api/gateways/openai_compat_stream.go): when streaming a response that will include tool-result injections, the gateway now waits for mergepoint assignment and streams slot-wrapped fragments to clients. We added a lightweight client-side marker in TS SDK so OpenAI-compat clients can present partial structured frames while preserving tool-call streaming expectations. 5) SDK ergonomic surface updates (redwood-sdk-typescript/src/tool-coalesce.ts): a new helper wraps parallel tool calls in a coalesce() helper so application code can opt into the grace-window behavior explicitly (default opt-out). 6) Tests and telemetry: integration tests added for high-concurrency tool scenarios, unit tests for mergepoint sequencing, and new metrics (invoker.coalesce.window_ms, invoker.mergepoint.latency, invoker.retry.quorum_falls) for Console dashboards. Backwards compatibility: default behavior is opt-out via the coalesce helper; internal routing will enable coalescing for customer-tiered policies where cost/perf tradeoffs favor batching (Dedicated plans). For users relying on immediate dispatch, new request flag coalesce=false bypasses the grace window. Rollout plan: feature gated behind runtime flag 'invoker_coalesce_v1'. Start with internal dogfood and canary on perf-canary cluster for 48 hours, then staged enablement on 10% of hosted traffic. Monitoring and observability: dashboards added to the 'invoker' tab in Redwood Console to track coalesce rate, average delay introduced, and any observed regression in percentiles for end-to-end token latency. Risks and mitigations: - Small added latency due to the grace window; mitigated by short default and per-request override. - Complex retry semantics could hide transient failures; mitigated by alerts for quorum_falls and automatic disabling if threshold crossed. Test plan: - unit tests for coalescer (edge cases: zero grace, long tail arrivals) - integration tests simulating 200 concurrent tool calls per request - streaming handshake tests with OpenAI-compat client simulation - load test comparing cost/requests before/after merge. Documentation: docs/api/tool-coalescing.md and an SDK example added in redwood-quickstarts/tool-coalescing-example. Related: builds on earlier tooling around deterministic injection and complements ENG-9047 (structured output regressions), ENG-8921 (streaming jitter incident).
Ravi Patel: Nice direction. A few questions about the retry quorum---can we avoid a blocker where the quorum holder dies before finishing and leaves a duplicate side-effect?
Ava Martinez: We added a short-lived lease with automatic failover; the coordinator will escalate to a quorum-based owner after lease expiry. Added tests to simulate owner crash (see test_invoker_coalesce.py).
Lin Chen: For OpenAI streaming compatibility, are clients expected to upgrade SDK? What's the plan if they don't?
Ava Martinez: Streaming format remains backward compatible. The SDK helper gives better ergonomic surface but gateway emits slot-wrapped fragments that degrade gracefully: older clients will receive plain text stream with injected payloads; new clients get structured frames. Documented in docs/api/tool-coalescing.md.
Marta Kowalska: Performance numbers? I want to make sure P99 doesn't regress for chat workloads.
Ava Martinez: Added perf-canary results in the PR: median increased by 2ms, P95 by 6ms, P99 unchanged within noise. Included flags to disable per-request. See telemetry/dashboards/invoker_coalesce.json.
Ravi Patel: Suggest tightening default grace window to 5ms for hosted tier to be conservative.
Ava Martinez: Reduced default to 10ms for Dedicated and 5ms for Hosted in subsequent commit and added runtime policy to override.
Introduces an opt-in coalescing invoker that batches closely timed tool calls using a short grace window to reduce duplicate warmups and stabilize streaming handshakes. Adds deterministic mergepoints for ordered tool-result injection and a coordinated retry token to minimize duplicate side-effects. Opt-in via SDK helper or request flag; gated rollout.
